content.text: decorator eliminates the repetitive boilerplate that otherwise clutters data-holding classes — the constructor, the repr, equality checks — while preserving full type-annotation support and adding powerful options like frozen (immutable) instances, post-construction validation, and one-call serialization to dicts. In agentic-AI systems, where dozens of structured types flow through every request cycle — tool invocations, trace spans, configuration objects, LLM response records — dataclasses are the lightweight, zero-dependency way to give that data a clear, self-documenting shape.
A dataclass is a regular Python class decorated with
(added in Python 3.7). The decorator reads the class body's type annotations and automatically generates several dunder methods: at minimum
. With additional flags it can also generate
and prevent mutation entirely. The result is a class that behaves like a well-written hand-coded data holder but requires far less typing.
The key distinction from plain classes is that field declarations are written as annotated class attributes, optionally with a default value or a
descriptor. The decorator then synthesises the actual implementation. No metaclass magic, no base class, no third-party dependency: the entire feature lives in the Python standard library.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    name: str                           # required positional argument
    args: dict                          # required positional argument
    call_id: str = ""                   # optional with default
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # mutable default

# Auto-generated __init__ lets you write:
tc = ToolCall(name="web_search", args={"q": "agents"})
print(tc)  # ToolCall(name='web_search', args={'q': 'agents'}, call_id='', tags=[])
print(tc == ToolCall(name="web_search", args={"q": "agents"}))  # True

Agentic pipelines shuttle richly structured data at every step: an LLM produces JSON describing a tool invocation; that JSON is parsed into a structured record; the record is dispatched to a handler; the result is wrapped in a trace span; the span is logged. Each of those boundaries could be an untyped dict, but dicts offer no field guarantees, no IDE autocompletion, no clear indication of which keys are required, and no meaningful repr for debugging.
Hand-written classes solve these problems but impose a tax: for every data class you write an

that compares them. For a ten-field record that is forty lines of code that must be kept in sync whenever the schema evolves. Dataclasses eliminate that maintenance burden entirely while keeping the type annotations that power static analysis tools.

The practical benefit shows up immediately in agent codebases: schema changes are one-line edits to the class body, repr output is always accurate, and tests can use simple equality comparison instead of field-by-field assertions.
is generated from annotations; adding a field requires exactly one line.
includes every field with its name and value, which is invaluable during debugging.
compares all fields, so two records with identical content are equal without any extra code.
— the class becomes hashable (usable as a dict key or in a set) and any mutation attempt raises
— prevents the classic Python mutable-default bug where all instances share the same list object.
— recursive conversion to dicts and tuples, ready for JSON encoding or database writes.
— no pip install, no version conflicts, no startup overhead.
— annotations are hints. Passing a string where an int is declared raises no error at construction time. Use Pydantic or
with validators when runtime guarantees are required.
— a parent dataclass with fields that have defaults followed by a child dataclass that adds required fields raises a
. Required fields must always precede optional ones, which conflicts with natural subclassing.
covers simple invariants but becomes unwieldy for cross-field rules, coercions, or conditional required fields. Pydantic's validator model is more expressive for those cases.
Frozen instances cannot hold mutable containers safely
prevents replacing a list field, but the list itself remains mutable. True deep immutability requires storing tuples instead.
— unlike Pydantic, dataclasses do not generate JSON Schema or OpenAPI specs. Tool definitions for LLM function-calling need a separate schema description.
When Python executes the decorated class body,
receives the class object. It iterates over

(the ordered dict of field name to type built by the interpreter), determines which fields have defaults and which do not, and constructs Python source strings for each generated method. Those strings are passed to
and the resulting function objects are attached directly to the class. This happens once at class definition time, not on every instantiation, so the runtime cost is negligible.
declares a required field. An annotation with an assignment like
declares an optional field with a default. When the default is a mutable object such as a list or dict, the assignment must use
instead — otherwise all instances would share the same object. The
to hide a field from the repr,
to exclude it from equality checks, and
to exclude it from the hash.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class AgentConfig:
    model: str = "gpt-4o"
    temperature: float = 0.7
    max_tokens: int = 4096
    stop_sequences: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    # metadata is excluded from repr and equality comparisons
    _created_at: float = field(default=0.0, repr=False, compare=False)

cfg = AgentConfig()
cfg2 = AgentConfig()
print(cfg == cfg2)   # True — _created_at is excluded from comparison
print(cfg)
# AgentConfig(model='gpt-4o', temperature=0.7, max_tokens=4096, stop_sequences=[])
after all fields have been assigned. It is the correct place to enforce invariants, normalise values, or derive computed fields.
recursively converts a dataclass instance to a plain dict, including any nested dataclass fields.
does the same into a tuple. Both are the standard path to JSON serialization without pulling in an ORM or serialization library.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict, astuple
import json

@dataclass
class ToolResult:
    tool_name: str
    output: str
    duration_ms: float
    error: str | None = None

result = ToolResult(tool_name="web_search", output="...", duration_ms=340.2)

# Serialize to JSON
payload = json.dumps(asdict(result))
print(payload)
# {"tool_name": "web_search", "output": "...", "duration_ms": 340.2, "error": null}

# Unpack as tuple (useful for CSV/database rows)
row = astuple(result)
print(row)  # ('web_search', '...', 340.2, None)
. The instance becomes hashable: it can be stored in a set, used as a dict key, or added to a
-decorated function's argument cache. This is the correct choice for immutable event records and cache-key objects.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class SpanKey:
    trace_id: str
    span_id: str

# Usable as a dict key or set member
seen: set[SpanKey] = set()
key = SpanKey(trace_id="t-001", span_id="s-007")
seen.add(key)

# Mutation raises an error immediately
try:
    key.trace_id = "new"
except Exception as e:
    print(type(e).__name__, e)
# FrozenInstanceError cannot assign to field 'trace_id'

decorator does at class definition time, and how the resulting typed instances move through a typical agentic pipeline — from raw LLM JSON output through dispatch and serialization.

content.text: Dataclasses are the right tool when the class's primary role is to hold data — when named, typed fields, a readable repr, and equality comparison are needed, but runtime validation, schema generation, or ORM integration are not.
Typical use cases in agentic systems:
— structured representation of the name, arguments, and call-id of a single LLM tool invocation.
— model name, temperature, token limits, stop sequences, tool list.
— structured log entries with name, timestamp, latency, and status. Use
so they are immutable after creation.
— wrap the raw API dict in a typed class for safe access and easy testing.
— each step in a multi-stage chain can exchange a well-typed dataclass rather than an untyped dict.
— if the data comes from an untrusted source (HTTP request body, user prompt, third-party API), use Pydantic. It validates types and coerces values at construction time.
JSON Schema or OpenAPI spec generation is needed
— LLM function-calling tool definitions need a machine-readable schema. Pydantic's
produces it automatically; dataclasses do not.
— cross-field constraints, conditional required fields, and coercions are awkward in
The following steps walk through building a production-quality dataclass layer for an agent's tool-dispatch subsystem.
from the standard library. No pip install needed.
Declare each field as an annotated class attribute. Required fields (no default) must come before optional fields (with defaults).
for any mutable default (list, dict, set).
for invariant checks or computed derived fields.
to records that must be immutable or hashable.
when the instance needs to be serialized to JSON or passed to a function expecting a plain dict.
A concise example showing the essential features: field declarations, a mutable default, and equality comparison.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class SearchQuery:
    query: str
    max_results: int = 5
    filters: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

q1 = SearchQuery(query="agentic AI")
q2 = SearchQuery(query="agentic AI")

print(q1)        # SearchQuery(query='agentic AI', max_results=5, filters=[])
print(q1 == q2)  # True  — structural equality for free

q1.filters.append("news")
print(q2.filters)  # []  — each instance has its own list (factory called per-instance)
Example 2 — Trace span with validation and serialization
A more realistic example modelling a distributed-tracing span, with post-init validation, frozen mode, and round-trip JSON serialization.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from enum import Enum
import json
import time

class SpanStatus(str, Enum):
    OK      = "ok"
    ERROR   = "error"
    TIMEOUT = "timeout"

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Span:
    trace_id:   str
    span_id:    str
    name:       str
    start_ns:   int
    end_ns:     int
    status:     SpanStatus = SpanStatus.OK
    attributes: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        if self.end_ns < self.start_ns:
            raise ValueError("end_ns must not precede start_ns")

    @property
    def duration_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.end_ns - self.start_ns) / 1_000_000

def record_span(name: str, **attrs) -> Span:
    t0 = time.time_ns()
    time.sleep(0.001)  # simulate work
    return Span(
        trace_id="t-001",
        span_id=f"s-{name[:4]}",
        name=name,
        start_ns=t0,
        end_ns=time.time_ns(),
        attributes=attrs,
    )

span = record_span("tool.web_search", query="agentic AI", results=10)
print(f"{span.name}  {span.duration_ms:.2f} ms  {span.status.value}")

# Serialize — normalise Enum value for JSON
d = asdict(span)
d["status"] = span.status.value
print(json.dumps(d, indent=2))

# Hashable — usable as dict key
cache: dict[Span, str] = {span: "cached"}

Production agent frameworks such as OpenAI's Agents SDK, LangGraph, and the Anthropic Claude API client all use structured records internally for tool calls, message turns, and run state. The pattern below mirrors the shape used by real frameworks: a typed tool-call dispatcher that parses LLM output, executes the right handler, and wraps the result in a typed response record.
The most common mistake is assigning a literal list or dict as a default value. Python evaluates class bodies once, so every instance would share the same object. The
decorator actually detects this and raises a
at class definition time, but the underlying bug is important to understand.

# WRONG — @dataclass raises ValueError at class definition time
# @dataclass
# class Bad:
#     items: list = []  # ValueError: mutable default is not allowed

# WRONG — same bug in a plain class (no error, silent sharing)
class AlsoBad:
    items = []  # shared across ALL instances!

a, b = AlsoBad(), AlsoBad()
a.items.append("x")
print(b.items)  # ['x']  — oops!

# RIGHT — field(default_factory=list)
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class Good:
    items: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

a, b = Good(), Good()
a.items.append("x")
print(b.items)  # []  — separate list per instance
Type annotations on dataclass fields are hints for static analysers like mypy and pyright. Python does not enforce them at runtime, so passing the wrong type silently creates an inconsistent object.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Config:
    max_tokens: int

cfg = Config(max_tokens="1024")   # No error at runtime!
print(type(cfg.max_tokens))       # <class 'str'>

# This will fail later — not at construction:
# result = cfg.max_tokens + 1    # TypeError

# Correct approach for untrusted data: Pydantic coerces and validates
from pydantic import BaseModel

class SafeConfig(BaseModel):
    max_tokens: int

safe = SafeConfig(max_tokens="1024")   # Coerced to int(1024)
print(type(safe.max_tokens))           # <class 'int'>
When a parent dataclass has optional fields (with defaults) and a child dataclass adds required fields (without defaults), Python raises a
at class-definition time because required arguments cannot follow optional ones in the generated
. Prefer composition over inheritance to avoid this entirely.
Frozen with mutable container — false sense of immutability
for every mutable default — list, dict, or set. Never assign a literal mutable collection directly.
immediately if the input violates a constraint.
to records that represent immutable events, cache keys, or anything stored in sets or used as dict keys.
to create a modified copy of a frozen instance rather than trying to mutate it.
— particularly useful for timestamps, internal IDs, or cache slots.
when building dataclass hierarchies. Inheritance interacts poorly with field ordering.
for class-level attributes that should NOT become constructor parameters.
Keep dataclasses as pure data holders
— heavy business logic belongs in service classes that accept dataclass instances as arguments.
Dataclass overhead is negligible. Method generation happens once at class-definition time via
is a normal Python function, not a wrapper, so it runs at the same speed as a hand-written constructor.
For memory footprint, each dataclass instance stores its fields as regular Python instance attributes in
and reduces memory usage by roughly 40–60 % for small objects. This matters when constructing millions of span records or tool-call objects during a large batch run.

from dataclasses import dataclass
import sys

@dataclass
class WithDict:
    a: int
    b: str

@dataclass(slots=True)      # Python 3.10+
class WithSlots:
    a: int
    b: str

d = WithDict(1, "x")
s = WithSlots(1, "x")

print(sys.getsizeof(d.__dict__))  # ~200+ bytes
print(hasattr(s, "__dict__"))     # False — no __dict__ overhead
performs a recursive traversal and dict-copy, which is fine for request-level records but can be a bottleneck if called millions of times per second. In that case, use a custom
method that avoids recursion, or switch to a library like
which generates optimised converters from the class schema.
are safe to use as keys in
, enabling constant-time deduplication of large event streams — a common requirement in tracing and analytics pipelines.
Both mypy and pyright fully understand
signature from field annotations and report type errors on construction sites. Running
on an agent codebase that uses dataclasses catches mismatched argument types, missing required fields, and incorrect attribute accesses at development time.

is a lightweight third-party library that generates optimised structuring (dict to dataclass) and unstructuring (dataclass to dict) functions. It handles nested dataclasses, unions, and Enum fields correctly, and the generated code is faster than

# pip install cattrs
import cattrs
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    name: str
    arguments: dict
    call_id: str

raw = {"name": "search", "arguments": {"q": "agents"}, "call_id": "c-1"}

# Structure (validates types during conversion)
tc = cattrs.structure(raw, ToolCall)
print(tc)  # ToolCall(name='search', arguments={'q': 'agents'}, call_id='c-1')

# Unstructure back to dict
print(cattrs.unstructure(tc))  # {'name': 'search', 'arguments': {...}, 'call_id': 'c-1'}
automatically, reducing per-instance memory by eliminating
— all fields become keyword-only arguments in
, eliminating positional-argument confusion in classes with many fields.
(default True) — enables structural pattern matching with
Internal typed records, agent state, events
API boundaries, LLM tool schemas, external data
Typing existing dicts; no runtime object created
Simple positional records; tuple API needed
Quick ad-hoc data; schema not yet needed

Dataclasses themselves introduce no security surface area — they are a code-generation tool that runs at import time and produces ordinary Python classes. The reliability considerations are about how they are used in production agent systems.

The most important production rule is to validate external data before constructing a dataclass from it. A dict arriving from an LLM response or an HTTP request may be missing fields, have wrong types, or contain unexpected extra keys. A direct
on missing required fields and silently accept wrong types. Use a constructor classmethod that validates the raw dict first:

Frozen dataclasses improve reliability in concurrent systems. When multiple coroutines reference the same span or tool-call record, immutability eliminates a class of race-condition bugs without requiring locks or defensive copying.

For high-reliability systems, combine the classmethod boundary check above with a Pydantic model at the outermost API layer. Pydantic handles coercion and schema errors gracefully; the internal dataclass then operates on already-validated, correctly-typed data, giving a clean separation between the untrusted boundary and the trusted interior.
Think of a dataclass as a

. The class body defines the column headers and their types. The decorator writes the formulas that fill in the missing cells (constructor, repr, equality). Each instance is one row — it has the exact columns declared, no more, no less.
locks the row so it cannot be edited after entry, making it safe to file in any folder (set or dict key).
exports the row to a plain Python dict, ready to be written to a JSON log or sent over the wire.
from type annotations, eliminating repetitive boilerplate for data-holding classes.
is mandatory for mutable defaults — assigning a literal collection directly shares it across all instances, a common and silent bug.
makes instances immutable and hashable; use it for event records, cache keys, and any object that must be stored in a set or used as a dict key.
is the hook for lightweight invariant validation after field assignment; for external or untrusted data, use Pydantic at the boundary instead.
provide zero-dependency recursive serialization to plain Python collections, ready for
and reduces memory by 40–60 %, significant at high throughput.
Dataclasses are not validators — type annotations are documentation only. Pair them with Pydantic at API and LLM-response boundaries where runtime coercion and schema generation matter.

In agentic pipelines, use dataclasses for tool-call records, configuration DTOs, trace spans, and intermediate pipeline results — anywhere a named, typed, self-describing structure replaces an opaque dict.
